figures.caption: Figure 2: Comparison of Helmholtz-SDE and the square root gauge on two OU spiral datasets: strong spiral, ω=2π (A-C), and no spiral, ω=0 (D). A: The symmetric KL distance (nats) to the posterior across observation noise and number of observations. B: The symmetric KL distance to the true prior. C: The dynamic learned by Helmholtz-SDE and the square root gauge against the true prior drift at noise σ = 0.3 and N = 10 observations. D: Same as B, but for the no spiral dataset.

Figure 3: Helmholtz-SDE closes the gap between simulation-based and simulation-free VI algorithms on the noisy Lorenz attractor. Negative ELBO and LL of true latents under the posterior are averaged over trials; sym KL is averaged over one-time marginals. Autocorr error is the entrywise RMSE of the global 3×3 autocorrelation matrix, averaged over horizon t=2. Errors are ±2SE over 5 replicates.

Figure 4: Helmholtz-SDE recovers predator-prey dynamics more faithfully than SDE Matching. A: Posterior mean ±2 SD over a representative window. B: Phase portraits of learned dynamics: Helmholtz-SDE recovers a limit cycle, SDE Matching decays to a fixed point. C: Peak-period distribution across 100-day forward simulations from the learned prior; dashed line marks the empirical period of 6.7 days. D: Rotation period, predator lag, and proportion of time in coherent rotation computed per replicate as the median across forward simulations using the wavelet analysis of Blasius et al. [54]; forecast MSE, in log coordinates. Values are median (IQR) across 10 replicates.

Figure 5: Helmholtz-SDE learns coherent wake dynamics in high-dimensional fluid flow past a 2D cylinder. A: True and forecasted mean velocity fields at 4 time units into the forecast window. B: Joint space–time autocorrelation along the streamwise direction in the vortex region [1, 10]× [−0.1, 0.1], from prior samples at the true t=0 initial condition. Diagonal banding indicates vortices being advected downstream at a coherent speed; rapid decay reflects loss of temporal coherence.
Figure 6: OU spiral with ω = 2π; top: symmetric KL to posterior; bottom: symmetric KL to prior.
Figure 7: OU spiral with ω = π; top: symmetric KL to posterior; bottom: symmetric KL to prior.
Figure 8: OU spiral with ω = π/2; top: symmetric KL to posterior; bottom: symmetric KL to prior.
Figure 9: OU spiral with ω = π/4; top: symmetric KL to posterior; bottom: symmetric KL to prior.
Figure 10: OU spiral with ω = 0; top: symmetric KL to posterior; bottom: symmetric KL to prior.

Figure 11: Visualization of three samples of stochastic Lorenz attractor dataset from Li et al. [2] (A) and this paper (B). Grey curves represent the true latent trajectory and grey × represent regularly-spaced observations. Colored curves represents the posterior mean and shading represents ±2 posterior standard deviations obtained from the iterated extended Rauch-Tung-Striebel smoother (Särkkä and Svensson [72, Chapter 13.3]). The Bayesian posterior in Li et al. [2] collapses to the true latent trajectory, whereas our dataset displays non-trivial posterior variance.
Table 1: Lobe-wise autocorrelation error for Helmholtz-SDE and the four baselines.

Figure 12: Three random samples from the true prior (A), the prior learned by Helmholtz-SDE (B), SDE Matching (C), and ARCTA/SVISE (D). Helmholtz-SDE more faithfully learns the dynamics, particularly the outward spiral dynamics within each lobe of the attractor.
